---
title: "Beer Markets Analysis"
author: "Aaron Zalen"
date: "2024"
format: 
  html:
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
    toc: true
    theme: cosmo
---

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| eval: true

library(tidyverse)
library(stargazer)
library(broom)
library(tidyverse)

beer_markets <- read_csv(
  "https://bcdanl.github.io/data/beer_markets_all_cleaned.csv"
)
```

## Beer Markets data analysis

This blog post will analyze the market for beer. We will explore how the price of beer varies by factors such as can size and region using regression models.

##### Descriptive statistics:

```{r}
colnames(beer_markets)
```

```{r}
summary <- beer_markets %>% 
  summarise(
    mean_price = mean(price_floz),
    mean_size = mean(beer_floz),
    mean_spent = mean(dollar_spent)
  )

summary
```

##### Filtering to only cans or non-refillable bottles

```{r}
beer_sub <- beer_markets %>% filter(container == "CAN" | container == "NON_REFILLABLE_BOTTLE")
```

##### Grouped summaries

```{r}
summarize_by_region <-  beer_markets %>% 
  group_by(region) %>% 
  summarise(
    mean_price = mean(price_floz),
    mean_size = mean(beer_floz),
    mean_spent = mean(dollar_spent)
  )

summarize_by_region
```

##### Regression models

```{r}
set.seed(123)

beer_markets <- beer_markets |> 
  mutate(rnd = runif(n()))

beer_train <- beer_markets |> 
  filter(rnd > 0.67)%>%
  mutate(market = as.factor(market),
         market = relevel(market, ref = "BUFFALO-ROCHESTER"))

beer_test <- beer_markets |> 
  filter(rnd <= 0.23)%>%
  mutate(market = as.factor(market),
         market = relevel(market, ref = "BUFFALO-ROCHESTER"))

nrow(beer_train) 
nrow(beer_markets)
```

```{r}
model1 <- lm(log(price_floz) ~ market + brand + I(container=="CAN") + log(beer_floz), 
             data = beer_train)

model2 <- lm(log(price_floz) ~ market + I(container=="CAN") + brand * log(beer_floz), 
             data = beer_train)

model3 <- lm(log(price_floz) ~ market + I(container=="CAN") + brand * promo * log(beer_floz), 
             data = beer_train)


```

```{r}
model1_pred_test <- augment(model1, newdata = beer_test)
model1_pred_test$pred_price_floz <- exp(model1_pred_test$.fitted)

model2_pred_test <- augment(model2, newdata = beer_test)
model2_pred_test$pred_price_floz <- exp(model2_pred_test$.fitted)

model3_pred_test <- augment(model3, newdata = beer_test)
model3_pred_test$pred_price_floz <- exp(model3_pred_test$.fitted)
```

```{r}
summary(model1_pred_test)
```

```{r}
summary(model2_pred_test)

```

```{r}
summary(model3_pred_test)
```

```{r}
rmse1 <- sqrt(mean((model1_pred_test$pred_price_floz - beer_test$price_floz)^2))
rmse2 <- sqrt(mean((model2_pred_test$pred_price_floz - beer_test$price_floz)^2))
rmse3 <- sqrt(mean((model3_pred_test$pred_price_floz - beer_test$price_floz)^2))
# View it
print(c(rmse1,rmse2, rmse3))
```

##### It looks like model three performs the best, being off at predicting beer price by about .849 dollars per ounce

```{r}
market_coefs <- tidy(model3) %>%
  filter(term %in% c("marketBUFFALO-ROCHESTER", "marketALBANY", "marketEXURBAN_NY", "marketRURAL_NEW_YORK", 
                     "marketsuburban_ny", "marketSYRACUSE", "marketURBAN_NY")) %>%
  select(term, estimate) %>%
  mutate(
    percent_diff = 100 * (exp(estimate) - 1)  # Exact percentage difference
  )
print(market_coefs)

```

#### Compared to buffalo-rochester:

######Albany is 4.37% more expensive. ExUrban NY is 11.1% more expensive. Rural NY is .817% cheaper

######Syracuse is 4.87% cheaper. Urban NY is 18.2% more expensive

**Answer:**

```{r}
# Model 1
model1$coefficients["log(beer_floz)"]

# Model 2
b2 <- tidy(model2) |> 
  filter(str_detect(term, "log\\(beer_floz\\)")) |> 
  select(term, elasticity = estimate)

b2

# Model 3
b3 <- coef(model3)
main <- b3["log(beer_floz)"]
promo_main <- b3["promoTRUE:log(beer_floz)"]

# Get brand names and terms
brands <- c("BUD_LIGHT", "BUSCH_LIGHT", "COORS_LIGHT", "MILLER_LITE", "NATURAL_LIGHT")
brand_terms <- b3[grepl("brand.*:log\\(beer_floz\\)$", names(b3))]
brand_promo_terms <- b3[grepl("brand.*:promo.*:log\\(beer_floz\\)", names(b3))]

promo_false_values <- numeric(5)
promo_true_values <- numeric(5)

promo_false_values[1] <- main
promo_true_values[1] <- main + promo_main

for(i in 2:5) {
  promo_false_values[i] <- main + brand_terms[i-1]
  promo_true_values[i] <- main + brand_terms[i-1] + promo_main + brand_promo_terms[i-1]
}

results <- data.frame(
  brand = brands,
  promo_false = promo_false_values,
  promo_true = promo_true_values
)

results$difference <- results$promo_true - results$promo_false
results
```

#######The coefficient for log(beer_floz) is -.1447708, which means that a 1% increase in fluid ounces is associated with a -.145% decrease in price per fluid ounce.

#######Compared to bud_light, busch light is .0031% more price sensitive. Coors_ligt is .0037% less price sensitive, Miller_light is .0161% more price sensitive, and Natural_light is .0585% less price sensitive

#######Promotion does seem to have an effect on price sensitivity, but it varies by brand. For instance, Bud light barely changes in price sensitivity when they advertise. Coors light however becomes more price sensitive by .052% compared to not promoting.

```{r}
model1_pred_test %>% 
  ggplot(aes(x = .fitted, y = .resid)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.25) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE, method = "loess") +
  labs(
    x = "Fitted values",
    y = "Residuals",
    title = "Model 1"
  )

model2_pred_test %>%  
  ggplot(aes(x = .fitted, y = .resid)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.25) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE, method = "loess") +
  labs(
    x = "Fitted values",
    y = "Residuals",
    title = "Model 2"
  )

model3_pred_test %>%  
  ggplot(aes(x = .fitted, y = .resid)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.25) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_smooth(se = FALSE, method = "loess") +
  labs(
    x = "Fitted values",
    y = "Residuals",
    title = "Model 3"
  )
```

####based on the residuals, it isn't clear which model performs the best. They all perform well, and have homoscedastisticty, with some major outliers where each model underrepresented the price by a large margin. All models look close to being centered around 0.

####I would prefer to use model 3. Model 3's RMSE of .008489 is the lowest out of all models. Additionally, the residual plot is on par with all other models.
